title: What is Angular and how does it differ from AngularJS?
description: Explain what Angular is and the key differences from the original AngularJS.
answer: **Angular** (2+) is a TypeScript-based, component-driven SPA framework. Unlike **AngularJS** (1.x, JavaScript, controllers/$scope, two-way digest cycle), modern Angular uses components + modules, a hierarchical DI system, RxJS observables, and ahead-of-time compilation — making it faster, more modular, and strongly typed.

title: What are components and modules in Angular?
description: Describe the role of components and NgModules.
answer: A **component** controls a piece of UI via a template, a TypeScript class, and styles (declared with `@Component`). A **module** (`@NgModule`) groups related components, directives, pipes, and providers into a cohesive block and declares what it exports/imports. Modern Angular also supports **standalone components** that skip NgModules.

title: What are the types of data binding in Angular?
answer: Four kinds: **interpolation** `{{value}}` and **property binding** `[prop]` (component → view), **event binding** `(click)` (view → component), and **two-way binding** `[(ngModel)]` (both directions, sugar for property + event binding).

title: What is a directive and what are the types?
answer: A **directive** adds behavior to elements. **Components** are directives with a template. **Structural directives** (`*ngIf`, `*ngFor`) change DOM layout. **Attribute directives** (`ngClass`, `ngStyle`, custom) change appearance/behavior of an existing element.

title: How does Dependency Injection work in Angular?
answer: Angular has a hierarchical **injector** tree. You register **providers** (at root, module, or component level) and request dependencies via constructor parameters. The injector resolves and caches instances, enabling singletons (`providedIn: 'root'`) and scoped instances. This decouples classes from how their dependencies are created.

title: What are Angular lifecycle hooks?
description: Name the key lifecycle hooks and when they run.
answer: Hooks let you tap into a component's life: `ngOnChanges` (input changes), `ngOnInit` (after first inputs set), `ngDoCheck`, `ngAfterContentInit/Checked`, `ngAfterViewInit/Checked`, and `ngOnDestroy` (cleanup, unsubscribe). `ngOnInit` is the usual place for initialization that needs inputs.

title: What is the difference between the constructor and ngOnInit?
answer: The **constructor** runs when the class is instantiated — use it only for DI. **ngOnInit** runs after Angular has set the component's `@Input` properties, so it's the correct place for initialization logic that depends on inputs or for starting data fetches.

title: What is the difference between Observables and Promises?
answer: A **Promise** resolves once with a single value and isn't cancelable. An **Observable** is a lazy, cancelable stream that can emit many values over time and supports powerful operators (`map`, `switchMap`, `debounceTime`). Angular's HttpClient and EventEmitter return Observables.

title: What is a service and why use it?
answer: A **service** is a class (usually `@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })`) that holds reusable logic or state — data fetching, business rules, shared state — kept out of components. Components inject it via DI, keeping the UI thin and the logic testable and shareable.

title: What are pipes in Angular?
description: Explain pipes and pure vs impure.
answer: **Pipes** transform values in templates (`{{ date | date:'short' }}`). Built-ins include `date`, `currency`, `async`. **Pure** pipes (default) recompute only when the input reference changes — efficient; **impure** pipes run on every change detection. You can build custom pipes with `@Pipe`.

title: How does change detection work in Angular?
answer: Angular uses **Zone.js** to know when async events occur, then walks the component tree checking bindings. The default strategy checks every component each cycle. **OnPush** restricts checks to when an `@Input` reference changes, an event fires in the component, or an observable bound with `async` emits — greatly reducing work in large apps.

title: What is the difference between reactive and template-driven forms?
answer: **Template-driven** forms define logic in the template with directives (`ngModel`) — simple, good for basic forms. **Reactive** forms define the model in the component with `FormGroup`/`FormControl` — explicit, synchronous, easier to test and to do dynamic/complex validation. Reactive is preferred for non-trivial forms.

title: How does routing and lazy loading work in Angular?
description: Explain the Router and lazy loading.
answer: The **Router** maps URL paths to components via a route config. **Lazy loading** uses `loadChildren`/`loadComponent` to load a feature's bundle only when its route is visited, shrinking the initial bundle and speeding first load.

title: What is an HTTP interceptor?
answer: An **interceptor** implements `HttpInterceptor` and sits in the HttpClient pipeline, letting you transform every outgoing request or incoming response — e.g. attach auth tokens, add headers, log, retry, or handle errors globally — without touching each call site.

title: What are route guards in Angular?
answer: **Guards** control navigation: `CanActivate`/`CanActivateChild` (allow entry), `CanDeactivate` (confirm leaving), `CanMatch` (route matching). A **Resolver** pre-fetches data before a route activates so the component renders with data ready. Modern Angular uses functional guards/resolvers.

title: What is the difference between AOT and JIT compilation?
answer: **JIT** compiles templates in the browser at runtime — handy for development. **AOT** compiles templates during the build, so the browser ships pre-compiled code: faster startup, smaller bundle (no compiler shipped), and template errors caught at build time. AOT is the production default.

title: What is the async pipe and why is it useful?
answer: The **async pipe** (`observable$ | async`) subscribes to an Observable/Promise in the template, renders the latest value, and **automatically unsubscribes** when the component is destroyed — preventing memory leaks and removing manual subscription boilerplate.

title: What are @ViewChild and @ContentChild?
answer: **@ViewChild** queries an element/component/directive from the component's own template; **@ContentChild** queries content projected into it via `<ng-content>`. Both give a reference (after the relevant lifecycle hook) to call methods or read properties on the child.

title: What are Angular Signals?
description: Explain the modern signals reactivity model.
answer: **Signals** are a fine-grained reactivity primitive: `signal()` holds a value, `computed()` derives from signals, and `effect()` runs side effects when dependencies change. They enable precise, zoneless change detection — Angular re-renders only what actually depends on a changed signal, rather than checking the whole tree.

title: What are standalone components?
answer: **Standalone components** (`standalone: true`) declare their own template dependencies via an `imports` array and don't need to be declared in an NgModule. They simplify the mental model, reduce boilerplate, and are now Angular's recommended default for new apps.
